pattern_recognition: This problem falls under the category of counting inversions or order statistics. It signals the need to analyze the relative positioning of elements in an array, specifically focusing on how many elements are smaller and to the right of each element.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: For each element in the array, count how many elements to its right are smaller.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through each element and for each element, iterate through the elements to its right to count smaller elements.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use a modified merge sort to count smaller elements while sorting the array, achieving better time complexity.
approaches.algorithm: Implement a merge sort that counts how many elements are smaller during the merge step.

dry_run: Consider the input array [5, 2, 6, 1]. For the first element 5, the smaller elements to its right are 2 and 1 (count = 2). For 2, the smaller element is 1 (count = 1). For 6, there are no smaller elements (count = 0). For 1, there are no elements to its right (count = 0). Thus, the output counts array is [2, 1, 0, 0].
edge_cases: An empty input should return an empty counts array. A single element input should return an array with a single zero. Duplicates should be handled correctly, counting only distinct smaller elements. Large inputs should be tested for performance, and care should be taken to avoid overflow in counting.
common_pitfalls: Common mistakes include: 1) Not considering elements that are equal when counting smaller elements. 2) Failing to handle the case of an empty array properly. 3) Using an inefficient algorithm that leads to timeouts on larger inputs.
